content.text: Subscriptions and event listeners are the classic use case for

with a cleanup function. The pattern is always the same: set up the listener or subscription when the effect runs, and tear it down in the returned cleanup function. Getting this right prevents memory leaks, stale callbacks, and duplicate handlers.

attrs.alt: Effect lifecycle: subscribe on mount, unsubscribe on cleanup

. If the function reference changes between renders, the old listener is never removed.

The function reference must be stable across the cleanup and the setup. Defining the handler inline inside the effect ensures the same function object is used for both add and remove. Moving it outside the effect would risk reference mismatch if the function were re-created on every render.
Listeners That Close Over Changing Values
Event handlers often close over state or props. When the closed-over value changes, the handler needs to be re-registered to capture the new value. Add each closed-over value to the dependency array.
changes, the cleanup fires — removing the old listener — and the effect fires again, adding a fresh listener that closes over the new
in the dependency array, the handler would always use the initial value of
as a dependency. The updater form always receives the current value — no need to close over it.
Observable libraries (RxJS, etc.) follow the same subscribe/unsubscribe lifecycle. The
call returns a subscription object whose

WebSocket connections are long-lived subscriptions that should be opened once and closed on cleanup. Any message handling is set up on the socket object itself, not in separate event listeners registered repeatedly.
returns a media query list that fires change events when the viewport crosses a breakpoint. This is useful for driving responsive logic in JavaScript without CSS.
is the modern, performant way to observe element size changes. It requires explicit disconnect in the cleanup to avoid observing a removed element.
every subscription or listener added in an effect must be removed in its cleanup function.
A missing cleanup means the listener or subscription outlives the component, accumulates across mounts, and calls
Strict Mode as a Leak Detector

React 18 Strict Mode unmounts and remounts every component once in development. This is deliberately designed to expose missing cleanups. If you see event listener callbacks firing twice or subscriptions doubling up in development, the cleanup is incomplete.
Wrapping a repeated listener pattern in a custom hook keeps component code clean and centralises cleanup:
The hook registers one listener per event type, cleaned up automatically when the component unmounts or the target / event / handler changes. Component code does not see any

Storing the handler in a ref lets the inner listener always call the latest version of the handler without re-registering the event listener. The ref is updated on every render by the first effect; the second effect only re-runs when the event name or target changes.

When an effect needs several listeners (e.g., both keydown and keyup), register all of them and remove all of them in a single cleanup function.
accepts a third argument: an options object or a boolean for capture phase. When adding passive listeners (for scroll performance) or capture-phase listeners, pass the same options to
— otherwise the removal silently fails in some browsers.
Marking a scroll or touch listener as passive tells the browser it will not call
, allowing it to optimise scrolling. The options must be passed to
too — omitting them removes the non-passive version, which may not exist.
Subscriptions and event listeners follow a strict setup/teardown lifecycle in
. The cleanup function is not optional — it is the mechanism that prevents leaks.
every addEventListener must have a matching removeEventListener with the same reference.
define the handler inside the effect so add and remove use the same function object.
add closed-over values to the dep array; the listener re-registers automatically.
open in effect, close in cleanup; reconnect by adding the target to the dep array.
double-mount in dev deliberately exposes missing cleanups.
useEventListener extracts the pattern and keeps component code clean.
use onClick/onChange for React elements; useEffect only for external targets.
